does: This paper presents methods for adversaries to remotely erase backdoors they have injected into federated learning models, enabling them to remove evidence of attack after achieving their malicious objectives. The authors propose unlearning-based strategies that allow backdoor removal while preserving the performance of the centralized model and avoiding over-unlearning, which could introduce detectable artifacts. The core contribution is demonstrating that backdoors in FL systems are not necessarily persistent threats—adversaries can execute a clean-up phase that eliminates backdoor functionality from the shared model while maintaining its utility on benign tasks, thus avoiding detection or penalty from the central server.
connects: This work builds on recent research demonstrating backdoor vulnerabilities in federated learning systems, where distributed participants can poison the global model through malicious updates. The paper extends the machine unlearning literature, which has primarily focused on legitimate privacy-driven model forgetting, to an adversarial setting where unlearning becomes an offensive tool. It responds to the implicit assumption in prior FL security work that backdoors remain detectable once embedded in models, and connects to broader research on the tension between model persistence and detectability in distributed machine learning attacks, where adversaries must balance effectiveness against discovery risk.
enables: This research highlights a new attack surface in federated learning security: the ability to remove evidence of compromise, making backdoor attacks more stealthy and harder to attribute. It raises urgent questions about verification mechanisms and audit trails in FL systems that can detect unlearning-based erasure attempts. The work enables future research into detection methods for suspicious unlearning patterns, defenses that make backdoor removal costly or impossible, and fundamental questions about model transparency and reproducibility when participants can selectively forget training contributions. It also motivates investigation into whether other forms of model tampering or data poisoning can be similarly erased in collaborative learning settings.
